Furthermore, the estimated crosscorrelation variable R h ( k ) ϵ (linear layers) and R g ( k ) ( H ( k,p ) ) ϵ (convolutional layers) are initialized with zero mean normal distributions with standard deviations σ R lin and σ R conv respectively.
texts.text: We use the Kaiming normal initialization for the weights, with a common standard deviation scaling parameter σ W , on both the linear and convolutional layers. Furthermore, the estimated crosscorrelation variable R h ( k ) ϵ (linear layers) and R g ( k ) ( H ( k,p ) ) ϵ (convolutional layers) are initialized with zero mean normal distributions with standard deviations σ R lin and σ R conv respectively.

texts.orig: I.6 Implementation details for Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA) and backpropagation training
texts.text: I.6 Implementation details for Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA) and backpropagation training

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This section presents further details on the numerical experiments comparing Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA) and Backpropagation (BP) methods, conducted under the same training conditions and number of epochs as those used for our proposed EBD algorithm. The results of these experiments are provided in Table 1 . We also include the DFA+E method, which extends DFA by incorporating correlative entropy regularization similar to the EBD. Note that, when the update on the R h ( k ) ϵ is fixed to its initialization, the EBD algorithm reduces to standard DFA.
texts.text: This section presents further details on the numerical experiments comparing Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA) and Backpropagation (BP) methods, conducted under the same training conditions and number of epochs as those used for our proposed EBD algorithm. The results of these experiments are provided in Table 1 . We also include the DFA+E method, which extends DFA by incorporating correlative entropy regularization similar to the EBD. Note that, when the update on the R h ( k ) ϵ is fixed to its initialization, the EBD algorithm reduces to standard DFA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For BP-based models trained on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, we used the Adam optimizer with hyperparameters β 1 = 0 . 9 , β 2 = 0 . 999 , and ϵ = 10 -8 [57]. For DFA and DFA+E models, we again used the Adam optimizer for CNN and LC models, while MLP models were trained using SGD with momentum.
texts.text: For BP-based models trained on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, we used the Adam optimizer with hyperparameters β 1 = 0 . 9 , β 2 = 0 . 999 , and ϵ = 10 -8 [57]. For DFA and DFA+E models, we again used the Adam optimizer for CNN and LC models, while MLP models were trained using SGD with momentum.

Some of the learning rate and the learning rate decay values or methodologies are linked to the tables corresponding to the hyperparameter details of its EBD counterpart, where the same method is also utilized for its DFA or DFA+E counterpart. Unlinked values denote a constant value applied to each layer, or the step decay multiplier applied per epoch. Additionally, sparsity inducing losses are not utilized for BP, DFA and DFA+E models.

texts.orig: I.7 Runtime comparisons for the update rules
texts.text: I.7 Runtime comparisons for the update rules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we present the relative average runtimes from the simulations, normalized to BP for the MNIST and CIFAR-10 models in Tables 20 and 21 respectively, for the models that we implemented and demonstrated their performance in Table 1.
texts.text: In this section, we present the relative average runtimes from the simulations, normalized to BP for the MNIST and CIFAR-10 models in Tables 20 and 21 respectively, for the models that we implemented and demonstrated their performance in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results show that entropy regularization in both EBD and DFA+E more than doubles the average runtime. However, these runtimes could be significantly improved by optimizing the implementation of the entropy gradient terms, specifically by avoiding repeated matrix inverse calculations. A more efficient approach would involve directly updating the inverses of the correlation matrices instead of recalculating both the matrices and their inverses at each step. This strategy aligns with the CorInfoMax-(EP/EBD) network structure. Nonetheless, we chose not to pursue this optimization, as CorInfoMax networks already employ it effectively.
texts.text: The results show that entropy regularization in both EBD and DFA+E more than doubles the average runtime. However, these runtimes could be significantly improved by optimizing the implementation of the entropy gradient terms, specifically by avoiding repeated matrix inverse calculations. A more efficient approach would involve directly updating the inverses of the correlation matrices instead of recalculating both the matrices and their inverses at each step. This strategy aligns with the CorInfoMax-(EP/EBD) network structure. Nonetheless, we chose not to pursue this optimization, as CorInfoMax networks already employ it effectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The efficiency of the DFA, DFA+E, and EBD methods can be further enhanced through low-level optimizations and improved implementations.
texts.text: The efficiency of the DFA, DFA+E, and EBD methods can be further enhanced through low-level optimizations and improved implementations.

texts.orig: I.9 Computational resources
texts.text: I.9 Computational resources

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All experiments were conducted within a High-Performance Computing (HPC) facility. Each experimental run utilized a single NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU equipped with 32GB of HBM2 memory. To provide context on execution times for our proposed CorInfoMax-EBD models:
texts.text: All experiments were conducted within a High-Performance Computing (HPC) facility. Each experimental run utilized a single NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU equipped with 32GB of HBM2 memory. To provide context on execution times for our proposed CorInfoMax-EBD models:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Training the 3-Layer CorInfoMax-EBD model (as described in Appendix I.2.5) for 30 epochs required approximately 22 hours.
texts.text: Training the 3-Layer CorInfoMax-EBD model (as described in Appendix I.2.5) for 30 epochs required approximately 22 hours.

texts.text: Training the 10-Layer CorInfoMax-EBD model (as described in Appendix I.2.6) for 100 epochs took approximately 75 hours. Execution times for other models (MLP, CNN, LC) and baseline methods were generally shorter; relative runtime comparisons are provided in Appendix I.7.

texts.text: J.1 Correlation in the mean squared error (MSE) criterion-based training

texts.orig: The MLP models are trained using the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer with a small learning rate of 10 -4 and the MSE criterion. In both plots, the initial value represents the correlation before training begins. The reduction in correlation observed during training provides insight into the core principle of the EBD algorithm.
texts.text: The MLP models are trained using the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimizer with a small learning rate of 10 -4 and the MSE criterion. In both plots, the initial value represents the correlation before training begins. The reduction in correlation observed during training provides insight into the core principle of the EBD algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To compute these correlations, we apply a batched version of Welford's algorithm [58], which efficiently calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient between hidden activations and errors in a memory-efficient way by using streaming statistics.
texts.text: To compute these correlations, we apply a batched version of Welford's algorithm [58], which efficiently calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient between hidden activations and errors in a memory-efficient way by using streaming statistics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Welford's algorithm works by accumulating the necessary statistics (e.g., sums and sums of squares) across batches of data and finalizing the correlation computation only after all data has been processed, avoiding the need to store all hidden activations simultaneously.
texts.text: Welford's algorithm works by accumulating the necessary statistics (e.g., sums and sums of squares) across batches of data and finalizing the correlation computation only after all data has been processed, avoiding the need to store all hidden activations simultaneously.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 10: The evolution of the average absolute correlation between layer activations and the error signal during backpropagation training of an MLP with two hidden layers (using the MSE criterion) on the MNIST dataset, showing the correlation decrease over epochs, on both the training and test sets.
texts.text: Figure 10: The evolution of the average absolute correlation between layer activations and the error signal during backpropagation training of an MLP with two hidden layers (using the MSE criterion) on the MNIST dataset, showing the correlation decrease over epochs, on both the training and test sets.

texts.orig: Given the hidden activations h ( k ) ∈ R b × N ( k ) , where b is the batch size and N ( k ) is the number of hidden units, and the errors ϵ ∈ R b × k , where k is the number of output dimensions (e.g., classes); the goal is to compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between activations h i for each hidden unit i and the corresponding error values across all samples as:
texts.text: Given the hidden activations h ( k ) ∈ R b × N ( k ) , where b is the batch size and N ( k ) is the number of hidden units, and the errors ϵ ∈ R b × k , where k is the number of output dimensions (e.g., classes); the goal is to compute the Pearson correlation coefficient between activations h i for each hidden unit i and the corresponding error values across all samples as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ρ ( k ) = Cov ( h ( k ) , ϵ ) √ σ 2 h ( k ) √ σ 2 ϵ + ϵ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where ϵ is a small constant for numerical stability. Finally, we compute the average of the absolute values of the correlation coefficients for each hidden layer k to generate the corresponding plots.
texts.text: where ϵ is a small constant for numerical stability. Finally, we compute the average of the absolute values of the correlation coefficients for each hidden layer k to generate the corresponding plots.

texts.orig: Figure 10 shows the correlation throughout training on the MNIST dataset, employing the MLP model detailed in Table 4, where we observe the correlation decay behavior similar to Figure 1c.
texts.text: Figure 10 shows the correlation throughout training on the MNIST dataset, employing the MLP model detailed in Table 4, where we observe the correlation decay behavior similar to Figure 1c.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To verify that the correlation-decay phenomenon observed for fully-connected networks extends to convolutional architectures, we trained a compact five-layer CNN consisting of three Conv+ReLU+ MaxPool blocks (with 32 , 64 , and 128 3 × 3 filters, respectively) followed by a fully-connected layer with 512 hidden units and an output layer with 10 logits. The network was optimized for 30 epochs on CIFAR -10 using mini-batch SGD ( η = 10 -3 , momentum 0 . 9 ) and the mean-squared error criterion on one-hot labels. After every forward pass we streamed the Pearson correlation between each hidden activation vector and the output error using the batched Welford estimator. Figure 11 plots the epoch-wise evolution of the average absolute correlation coefficient for both training and test data. As with the MLP in Figure 1c, all layers exhibit a pronounced monotonic decline, confirming that back-propagation progressively enforces the stochastic orthogonality of hidden activations and output errors in convolutional networks as well, as expected.
texts.text: To verify that the correlation-decay phenomenon observed for fully-connected networks extends to convolutional architectures, we trained a compact five-layer CNN consisting of three Conv+ReLU+ MaxPool blocks (with 32 , 64 , and 128 3 × 3 filters, respectively) followed by a fully-connected layer with 512 hidden units and an output layer with 10 logits. The network was optimized for 30 epochs on CIFAR -10 using mini-batch SGD ( η = 10 -3 , momentum 0 . 9 ) and the mean-squared error criterion on one-hot labels. After every forward pass we streamed the Pearson correlation between each hidden activation vector and the output error using the batched Welford estimator. Figure 11 plots the epoch-wise evolution of the average absolute correlation coefficient for both training and test data. As with the MLP in Figure 1c, all layers exhibit a pronounced monotonic decline, confirming that back-propagation progressively enforces the stochastic orthogonality of hidden activations and output errors in convolutional networks as well, as expected.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 11: The evolution of the average absolute correlation between layer activations and the error signal during back-propagation training of a CNN on CIFAR-10. The CNN contains three convolutional layers and two fully-connected layers and is trained with the MSE criterion. The correlation decreases over epochs on both the training and test sets, mirroring the behavior observed for the MLP architecture and supporting the generality of the correlation decay across architectures.
texts.text: Figure 11: The evolution of the average absolute correlation between layer activations and the error signal during back-propagation training of a CNN on CIFAR-10. The CNN contains three convolutional layers and two fully-connected layers and is trained with the MSE criterion. The correlation decreases over epochs on both the training and test sets, mirroring the behavior observed for the MLP architecture and supporting the generality of the correlation decay across architectures.

texts.orig: 1. Claims
texts.text: 1. Claims

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?
texts.text: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: The abstract and introduction claim EBD is a novel, MMSE-grounded framework for biologically plausible credit assignment, circumventing weight transport, leading to three-factor rules, integrating with CorInfoMax, and achieving competitive or better performance against other error-broadcast methods on benchmarks. These claims are supported by the theoretical development in Sections 2 and 3 (e.g., MMSE orthogonality, three-factor learning rule derivation in 3.1) and experimental results in Section 4 (Tables 1 and 2). The scope, including scalability as an area for further exploration, is also appropriately stated.
texts.text: Justification: The abstract and introduction claim EBD is a novel, MMSE-grounded framework for biologically plausible credit assignment, circumventing weight transport, leading to three-factor rules, integrating with CorInfoMax, and achieving competitive or better performance against other error-broadcast methods on benchmarks. These claims are supported by the theoretical development in Sections 2 and 3 (e.g., MMSE orthogonality, three-factor learning rule derivation in 3.1) and experimental results in Section 4 (Tables 1 and 2). The scope, including scalability as an area for further exploration, is also appropriately stated.

texts.orig: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs
texts.text: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?
texts.text: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: The theoretical grounding of EBD on the MMSE orthogonality principle is detailed in Section 2.2. Appendix A provides preliminaries on nonlinear MMSE estimation. Appendix B elaborates on the stochastic orthogonality condition, its sufficiency for MMSE optimality (Theorem B.1), and its application to network training, including arguments for using hidden unit activations under idealized conditions (Theorem B.2). These sections outline the assumptions and provide the theoretical arguments/proofs.
texts.text: Justification: The theoretical grounding of EBD on the MMSE orthogonality principle is detailed in Section 2.2. Appendix A provides preliminaries on nonlinear MMSE estimation. Appendix B elaborates on the stochastic orthogonality condition, its sufficiency for MMSE optimality (Theorem B.1), and its application to network training, including arguments for using hidden unit activations under idealized conditions (Theorem B.2). These sections outline the assumptions and provide the theoretical arguments/proofs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?

texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: Section 4 outlines the experimental setup. Appendix I ('Supplementary on Numerical Experiments') provides extensive details regarding network architectures (I.1), hyperparameters for all presented models (EBD variants and baselines like BP, DFA) across datasets (I.3-I.7), optimizers, learning rate schedules, and other implementation specifics necessary to understand and reproduce the experiments. We also provide all codes as supplementary to the article.
texts.text: Justification: Section 4 outlines the experimental setup. Appendix I ('Supplementary on Numerical Experiments') provides extensive details regarding network architectures (I.1), hyperparameters for all presented models (EBD variants and baselines like BP, DFA) across datasets (I.3-I.7), optimizers, learning rate schedules, and other implementation specifics necessary to understand and reproduce the experiments. We also provide all codes as supplementary to the article.

In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: Experiments were conducted five times, and average and standard deviation values are reported in Tables. Table 2 reports mean and standard deviation for the 10-layer CorInfoMax-EBD results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: All experiments utilized a single NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU with 32GB HBM2 memory, accessed via an HPC facility (details in Appendix I). Appendix I.9 provides specific execution time examples for the more computationally intensive CorInfoMax-EBD models. Appendix I.7 further provides relative runtime comparisons for other models and methods.

Appendix I.7 further provides relative runtime comparisons for other models and methods.

For example, it is legitimate to point out that an improvement in the quality of generative models could be used to generate deepfakes for disinformation. On the other hand, it is not needed to point out that a generic algorithm for optimizing neural networks could enable people to train models that generate Deepfakes faster.

texts.label: text
